h1Title: Junin Hemorrhagic Fever: Symptoms, Treatment & Prognosis
heroOverview: Junin hemorrhagic fever (JHF, also called Argentine hemorrhagic fever) is a severe rodent-borne viral haemorrhagic fever endemic to the pampas region of Argentina, caused by the Junin virus (a member of the Arenaviridae family, genus Mammarenavirus). The virus is maintained in nature in the drylands of central Argentina, transmitted to humans primarily through contact with the urine or excreta of the drylands cricetine rodent (Calomys musculinus) — the natural reservoir — or by inhalation of contaminated aerosol. JHF has an estimated annual incidence of 100-1,500 cases, with mortality rates of 15-30% in untreated patients. The disease presents with fever, malaise, haemorrhagic manifestations, and neurological involvement, following a biphasic course. Early recognition and treatment with intravenous ribavirin and, where available, immune plasma (convalescent plasma from recovered patients) substantially reduces mortality to 1-5%. Supportive care for haemorrhagic complications is equally critical. Containment and vector control measures reduce transmission risk in endemic areas.

keyStats.prevalence: Endemic to the pampas region of Argentina (Buenos Aires, Cordoba, Santa Fe, La Pampa provinces); 100-1,500 cases annually; seasonal peak during autumn harvest (March-July) when agricultural workers have peak rodent exposure
keyStats.demographics: Affects primarily agricultural workers (maize harvesters, field workers) aged 20-50; male predominance reflecting occupational exposure; occasional nosocomial transmission to healthcare workers
keyStats.avgAge: Median age at presentation 35-45 years; range 15-70 years
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 1,500-3,000 cumulative confirmed cases since first description in 1958; mortality 15-30% without treatment

definition: Junin hemorrhagic fever (ICD-10: A96.0, Argentine hemorrhagic fever) is a serious rodent-borne viral haemorrhagic fever caused by Junin virus (genus Mammarenavirus, family Arenaviridae). The virus is transmitted by direct contact with infected rodent excreta (urine, saliva) through broken skin or mucous membranes, or by inhalation of aerosolised contaminated material. The incubation period is 7-14 days. The disease follows a biphasic course: after a prodromal phase of fever, malaise, headache, and myalgia, patients develop haemorrhagic manifestations (gingival bleeding, petechiae, ecchymoses), leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, proteinuria, and neurological involvement (tremor, ataxia, seizures, coma). The haematological abnormalities (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia) are characteristic and distinguish JHF from other haemorrhagic fevers. Pathogenesis involves endothelial dysfunction, immune-mediated capillary leak, and consumptive coagulopathy. Diagnosis is confirmed by viral isolation, RT-PCR, or ELISA for virus-specific IgM. Treatment with intravenous ribavirin (first 6 days of illness) and immune plasma reduces mortality from 15-30% to 1-5%.

typesClassification.description: Approximately 30% of cases; fever and malaise without significant haemorrhagic manifestations; lower mortality with supportive care alone.
Approximately 40% of cases; haemorrhagic manifestations with thrombocytopenia and proteinuria; responds well to ribavirin and immune plasma.
Approximately 20% of cases; tremor, ataxia, seizures, and altered consciousness in addition to haemorrhagic features; high mortality without early treatment.

typesClassification.type: JHF with shock and multi-organ failure
typesClassification.description: Approximately 10% of cases; distributive shock, disseminated intravascular coagulation, renal failure, and hepatic involvement; very high mortality despite aggressive therapy.
Transmission to healthcare workers via needlestick or mucous membrane exposure to patient blood or secretions; often more severe due to higher inoculum.

primarySymptoms: High fever (38.5-40°C) with chills, rigors, and severe malaise that is disproportionate to the apparent severity of the illness.
Severe myalgia and headache, particularly retro-orbital pain and photophobia.
Non-specific prodromal symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, anorexia, and abdominal pain.
Gingival bleeding, haematemesis, and melaena indicating upper and lower gastrointestinal haemorrhage.
Petechiae on the trunk and limbs, conjunctival haemorrhage, and ecchymoses at injection sites.
Proteinuria and haematuria reflecting renal involvement; oliguria or anuria in severe cases.
Leukopenia (WBC <2,000/µL) and thrombocytopenia (<50,000/µL) on laboratory testing.
Neurological manifestations including tremor of the hands and tongue, ataxia, seizures, and progressive obtundation.
Facial flushing, conjunctival injection, and pharyngeal enanthem in the first week.
Progressive hypotension and shock in severe untreated cases.
earlyWarningSigns: Fever, malaise, and laboratory thrombocytopenia in a patient with recent exposure in the Argentine pampas (occupational or residential)
New haemorrhagic manifestations (gingival bleeding, petechiae, ecchymoses) in a febrile patient
Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia in a patient from an endemic area
Proteinuria with haematuria in a febrile patient
Neurological signs (tremor, ataxia) in a patient with fever and haemorrhagic features
Healthcare worker with fever and cytopenias following needlestick exposure to a patient with febrile illness from an endemic area
emergencySigns: Haematemesis or melaena indicating active upper or lower GI bleeding — requires urgent blood product transfusion and haemodynamic support
Seizures with progressive neurological deterioration — requires urgent anticonvulsant therapy and ICU-level care
Distributive shock with hypotension, tachycardia, and organ hypoperfusion — requires aggressive fluid resuscitation and vasopressor support
Respiratory failure requiring intubation from pulmonary haemorrhage or encephalopathy
Severe multi-organ failure with DIC, renal failure, and hepatic involvement — requires ICU-level care and blood product support

causes.cause: Junin virus infection from rodent excreta exposure
causes.description: JHF is caused by Junin virus transmitted via contact with the urine, saliva, or excreta of infected Calomys musculinus (drylands cricetine rodent), the natural reservoir. Transmission occurs through broken skin, mucous membranes, or inhalation of aerosolised contaminated material. The virus replicates in macrophages, dendritic cells, and endothelial cells, causing immune activation, cytokine release, and capillary leak.

causes.cause: Seasonal occupational exposure during maize harvest
causes.description: The majority of JHF cases occur during the autumn harvest (March to July) when agricultural workers in endemic provinces are exposed to high rodent populations invading harvested fields. Rodents shelter in stored maize and grain, contaminating the environment with urine containing virus.

Human-to-human transmission has been documented via needlestick injuries, mucous membrane exposure to patient blood or secretions, and inadequate infection control. Healthcare worker cases often have higher inoculum and more severe disease.

causes.cause: Inadequate infection control in endemic settings
causes.description: In endemic rural hospitals, limited resources for infection control, reuse of needles, and lack of isolation facilities facilitate nosocomial transmission. Improvement in hospital hygiene and isolation practices has reduced nosocomial transmission in recent decades.

causes.cause: Delay in seeking medical care in remote endemic areas
causes.description: Patients in remote rural areas may delay seeking care until advanced haemorrhagic manifestations develop, missing the therapeutic window for early ribavirin and immune plasma. Geographic barriers to healthcare contribute to high mortality.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational exposure during maize harvest in endemic provinces
riskFactors.description: Agricultural workers harvesting maize in the Argentine pampas during autumn months (March-July) have the highest risk. The risk increases with duration and intensity of field exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in endemic rural areas of central Argentina
riskFactors.description: People living in or near endemic provinces (Buenos Aires, Cordoba, Santa Fe, La Pampa) are at elevated risk through domestic or occupational contact with infected rodent populations.

riskFactors.factor: Healthcare worker handling JHF patients without adequate protection
riskFactors.description: Healthcare workers exposed to patient blood, secretions, or contaminated needles without proper PPE are at risk of nosocomial infection; strict isolation precautions are mandatory.
Male predominance in JHF reflects occupational exposure patterns (agricultural field work predominantly performed by men in these communities).

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate PPE when handling rodents or working in endemic fields
riskFactors.description: Workers without gloves, masks, and protective clothing when handling maize, grain, or rodent-contaminated materials are at elevated risk.

affectedDemographics: Primarily agricultural workers aged 20-50 in endemic Argentine provinces, particularly during the maize harvest season.
Male predominance (approximately 80% of cases) reflecting occupational exposure patterns.
Healthcare workers are at risk during nosocomial outbreaks if infection control measures are inadequate.
Children are rarely affected; when they are, disease is often milder.
Tourists visiting endemic areas during harvest season have a small but real risk.
diagnosisOverview: JHF should be suspected in any patient with fever, malaise, and laboratory findings of leukopenia and thrombocytopenia with recent exposure in the Argentine pampas, particularly during the harvest season. Initial work-up includes CBC with differential (leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, lymphocytosis), coagulation studies (PT/PTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer for DIC), urinalysis (proteinuria, haematuria), and LFTs. Specific diagnosis is confirmed by: RT-PCR on serum or blood (positive in first 7-10 days of illness); ELISA for Junin virus-specific IgM (positive from day 7 onwards); viral isolation from blood (requires BSL-4 facility); or immunohistochemistry on tissue samples. Differential diagnosis includes other viral haemorrhagic fevers (Machupo, Lassa, Ebola), severe influenza, meningococcaemia, and other causes of fever with haemorrhagic manifestations. A clinical case definition combining epidemiological exposure, fever, haemorrhagic manifestations, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia has high sensitivity for early case identification.

diagnosticTests.test: RT-PCR for Junin virus RNA in serum or blood
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapid specific diagnosis; positive in first 7-10 days of illness; sensitivity >95% in first week
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample; results in 24-48 hours at reference laboratory with BSL-4 capability

diagnosticTests.test: ELISA for Junin virus-specific IgM antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms diagnosis from day 7 onwards; IgM remains positive for months after recovery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood test; results in 1-3 days at reference laboratory

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characteristic findings: leukopenia (WBC <2,000/µL), thrombocytopenia (<50,000/µL), relative lymphocytosis; baseline for monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test; results within hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) in severe cases; guides blood product transfusion
Proteinuria and haematuria are characteristic; markers of renal involvement and disease severity
Elevated AST/ALT reflect hepatic involvement; transaminitis is common but jaundice is unusual
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood test; results within hours

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for definitive diagnosis; performed only at reference virology laboratories with BSL-4 capability
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood sample in special transport medium; results in 1-2 weeks

diagnosticTests.purpose: May show interstitial infiltrates in pulmonary haemorrhage; baseline for patients with respiratory symptoms

treatmentOverview: Treatment of JHF requires early initiation of specific antiviral therapy, aggressive supportive care, and strict infection control. Specific therapy with intravenous ribavirin (30 mg/kg loading dose, then 15 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days, then 7.5 mg/kg every 8 hours for 6 days) should be initiated as early as possible (ideally within the first 6 days of illness) and is most effective when combined with immune plasma. Convalescent immune plasma from recovered JHF patients (containing neutralising antibodies to Junin virus) is administered as early as possible at 20-40 mL/kg. Both treatments together reduce mortality from 15-30% to 1-5%. Supportive care is critical and includes: fluid management (cautious to avoid pulmonary oedema), blood product transfusion (packed RBCs, platelets, fresh frozen plasma for DIC), management of haemorrhage, seizure control, and prevention of secondary bacterial infection. Experimental therapies including favipiravir and monoclonal antibodies are under investigation. Strict isolation precautions (BSL-4 or contact/droplet precautions with careful barrier nursing) prevent nosocomial transmission. Ribavirin is also used prophylactically for high-risk exposures (needlestick from confirmed JHF patient).

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous ribavirin 30 mg/kg loading dose, then 15 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days, then 7.5 mg/kg every 8 hours for 6 days
medicalTreatments.description: Nucleoside analogue with broad antiviral activity; most effective when initiated in first 6 days of illness; penetrates the blood-brain barrier.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality by 50-60% when combined with immune plasma; reduces viraemia and disease duration

medicalTreatments.name: Convalescent immune plasma 20-40 mL/kg (Junin virus-neutralising antibodies)
medicalTreatments.description: Plasma from recovered JHF patients containing neutralising antibodies; administered as early as possible; most effective in first week.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality dramatically; efficacy depends on neutralising antibody titre and timing of administration

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive care: blood product transfusion (PRBC, platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate)
medicalTreatments.description: Aggressive transfusion support for haemorrhagic complications; guided by coagulation studies and clinical bleeding.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Life-saving in severe haemorrhage; maintains haemodynamic stability and oxygen delivery

medicalTreatments.name: Seizure management (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or midazolam)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard anticonvulsant therapy for JHF-associated seizures; rapid control prevents aspiration and secondary brain injury.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Seizure control in 80-90% of cases; refractory seizures require ICU sedation

medicalTreatments.name: Vasopressor support (noradrenaline or dopamine) for distributive shock
medicalTreatments.description: Initiated when fluid resuscitation is insufficient to maintain perfusion; target MAP >65 mmHg.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Maintains organ perfusion in distributive shock; requires ICU monitoring

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics for secondary bacterial infection
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical antibiotics for suspected bacterial sepsis, pneumonia, or line infection; common in severe JHF.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces mortality from secondary bacterial infection; choose agents covering Gram-negatives and Staph aureus

medicalTreatments.name: Postexposure ribavirin prophylaxis for high-risk exposures
medicalTreatments.description: Oral or IV ribavirin for healthcare workers with needlestick or mucous membrane exposure to JHF patient blood; should be initiated within 24 hours of exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces risk of clinical JHF after high-risk exposure; evidence from case series

medicalTreatments.name: Favipiravir (investigational, under clinical trial)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum antiviral with in vitro activity against Junin virus; clinical trials ongoing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Preclinical data promising; clinical efficacy unproven; available through compassionate use

surgicalOptions.name: Tracheostomy for prolonged mechanical ventilation
surgicalOptions.description: Performed in patients requiring prolonged intubation and ventilation; facilitates weaning and airway care.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Standard procedure in ICU setting; complications include infection and airway stenosis

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical control of life-threatening haemorrhage (gastrointestinal, surgical site)
surgicalOptions.description: Emergency surgical intervention for refractory haemorrhage; rarely needed due to effectiveness of interventional radiology and blood product support.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Depends on site and cause of haemorrhage; often a last resort in DIC

alternativeTreatments.name: Monoclonal antibody therapy (investigational)
alternativeTreatments.description: J199 and other neutralising monoclonal antibodies targeting Junin virus have shown promise in animal models and are under clinical development.

alternativeTreatments.name: Convalescent serum from recovered patients (alternative to plasma)
alternativeTreatments.description: Recovered patient serum with high neutralising antibody titre; used when immune plasma is unavailable.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive oxygenation and ventilation management
alternativeTreatments.description: Early intubation and protective ventilation strategy for patients with pulmonary haemorrhage or ARDS; prevents further ventilator-induced lung injury.

whySeeSpecialist: JHF requires urgent specialist management in a referral centre with infectious disease, virology, and critical care expertise. Early treatment with ribavirin and immune plasma dramatically reduces mortality and should be initiated at the first clinical suspicion before laboratory confirmation. Strict isolation protocols and coordination with public health authorities are essential to prevent nosocomial spread.
doctorSelectionGuide: In Argentina, the Referral Hospital for JHF is the Hospital Italiano in Buenos Aires and designated provincial hospitals in endemic areas. International cases require evacuation to a BSL-4-equipped facility. Ask about BSL-4 capability, ribavirin and immune plasma availability, and experience with viral haemorrhagic fevers.
hospitalCriteria: BSL-4 laboratory capability for Junin virus diagnostics (or access to reference laboratory)
ICU with capacity for viral haemorrhagic fever patients with full isolation
Access to intravenous ribavirin and immune plasma
Infectious disease and tropical medicine specialists with viral haemorrhagic fever experience
Blood bank with capacity for massive transfusion support
keyFacilities: High-containment isolation ward (BSL-4 equivalent) for JHF patients
Reference virology laboratory with RT-PCR and ELISA capability
Blood bank with blood product support (PRBC, platelets, FFP, cryoprecipitate)
ICU with full organ support capability (mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, CRRT)
Public health interface for case notification and contact tracing
preventionStrategies: Use of personal protective equipment (gloves, masks, gowns) when handling rodents, grain, or maize in endemic areas.
Rodent control in homes and work environments near endemic areas; trapping and exclusion.
Vaccination of at-risk agricultural workers with the live attenuated Junin virus vaccine (Candid#1) — used in Argentina for high-risk workers since 1990s.
Avoidance of endemic areas during the harvest season for non-immune individuals.
Strict infection control (standard and droplet precautions) in hospitals managing suspected JHF cases to prevent nosocomial transmission.
Contact tracing and post-exposure monitoring for household and healthcare worker contacts.
lifestyleModifications: Agricultural workers in endemic provinces should use PPE (gloves, masks, long-sleeved clothing) during harvest activities.
Seal homes to prevent rodent entry; store food in rodent-proof containers.
Seek medical attention promptly if fever develops after potential exposure in an endemic area.
Healthcare workers must use full PPE when managing patients with suspected JHF.
No specific dietary restrictions; maintain hydration and nutritional intake during the acute illness.

recommended: High-calorie diet to support recovery; protein intake of 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day
Frequent small meals if nausea and vomiting are present
Electrolyte replacement if diarrhoea or vomiting is profuse
IV nutrition if oral intake is insufficient
avoid: Raw or undercooked food from endemic areas (potential rodent contamination)
Aspirin or NSAIDs (increased bleeding risk in thrombocytopenic patients)

exerciseGuidelines: Rest is essential during the acute illness. Recovery from JHF takes several weeks; light activity may be resumed gradually as fever resolves and blood counts recover. Full return to normal activity typically takes 4-8 weeks. Avoid strenuous exercise for at least 4-6 weeks to allow haematological recovery.
dailyManagement: Strict isolation precautions to prevent nosocomial spread: patient in private room, gowns, gloves, masks, and eye protection for all visitors and staff.
Daily CBC, coagulation studies, and urinalysis to monitor disease progression and response to treatment.
Ribavirin administered on schedule (initiate as early as possible, ideally within first 6 days).
Blood product transfusion as indicated by coagulation studies and clinical bleeding.
Report new or worsening haemorrhage, neurological symptoms, or haemodynamic instability immediately to the treating team.
prognosis: With early treatment (ribavirin and immune plasma initiated within the first week of illness), mortality falls from 15-30% to 1-5%. Poorer prognostic factors include: delayed treatment (after day 7), neurological involvement, severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000/µL), high viral load, advanced age, and underlying comorbidities. Patients who survive typically recover completely over 4-8 weeks with normalisation of haematological parameters and resolution of organ involvement. Long-term sequelae are uncommon. Neurological sequelae (tremor, cognitive impairment) may persist in a small proportion of severe cases. Recovery generates neutralising antibodies providing lifelong immunity to Junin virus.
recoveryTimeline: Fever resolves in surviving patients by days 10-14 with appropriate treatment. Haematological parameters (WBC, platelets) begin to recover by days 14-21. Full haematological recovery is typically complete by weeks 4-8. Most patients are discharged from hospital 2-4 weeks after onset. Physical recovery continues for several weeks after discharge. Return to agricultural work is usually possible within 6-8 weeks, assuming full haematological recovery.
complications: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) with consumptive coagulopathy, severe bleeding, and multi-organ failure — managed with aggressive blood product support (FFP, cryoprecipitate, platelets); high mortality.
Neurological involvement (tremor, ataxia, seizures, coma) from encephalitis or cerebral haemorrhage — requires ICU care and anticonvulsant therapy; may cause long-term sequelae.
Severe haemorrhage (GI bleeding, pulmonary haemorrhage) requiring massive transfusion — managed with endoscopic or interventional radiology haemostasis and blood product support.
Secondary bacterial infection (pneumonia, line sepsis, UTI) in immunocompromised patients — requires broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion or direct viral nephrotoxicity — may require renal replacement therapy.
Pulmonary oedema from fluid overload or capillary leak — requires careful fluid management and diuretics.
Cardiac involvement (myocarditis, arrhythmias) — requires cardiac monitoring and supportive care.
Prolonged convalescence with persistent fatigue, cognitive impairment, and tremor in 5-10% of survivors.

supportResources.description: WHO information on viral haemorrhagic fevers including JHF, with management guidelines and surveillance data.

supportResources.description: US CDC information on viral haemorrhagic fevers including arenaviruses, with travel health notices and clinical guidance.

supportResources.name: PAHO — Junin virus and Argentine hemorrhagic fever

confusedWithConditions.name: Machupo Hemorrhagic Fever (Bolivian HF)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Machupo virus (also an Arenavirus) causes Bolivian haemorrhagic fever with similar clinical features but endemic to Bolivia. Geographical history and specific serology distinguish the two. Both respond to ribavirin and immune plasma.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lassa Fever
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lassa fever is caused by Mastomys natalensis rodent in West Africa; presents with fever, haemorrhage, and pharyngitis; common in West Africa, not Argentina. RT-PCR and serology distinguish the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ebola Virus Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ebola is endemic to West and Central Africa, transmitted by fruit bats; causes more severe and rapid haemorrhagic disease with higher mortality. Ebola and Junin virus are phylogenetically distinct and require different diagnostics.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hantavirus is transmitted by deer mice and causes pulmonary oedema with respiratory failure; less haemorrhagic than JHF and different epidemiology (Americas, Europe, Asia). Serology distinguishes the two.

relatedConditions.name: Machupo Hemorrhagic Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are South American arenaviral haemorrhagic fevers with similar clinical presentation, treatment (ribavirin, immune plasma), and prognosis. Both are rodent-borne. Distinction is by geography and specific serology.

relatedConditions.name: Lassa Fever
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common arenaviral haemorrhagic fever; shares treatment strategy with JHF (ribavirin, immune plasma). Different epidemiology and geography.

relatedConditions.name: Viral Haemorrhagic Fevers (General)
relatedConditions.relevance: JHF is one of several viral haemorrhagic fevers requiring BSL-4 containment, ribavirin, and immune plasma; the broader category informs infection control and public health responses.

faqs.question: What is Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Junin hemorrhagic fever is a severe viral disease endemic to the pampas region of Argentina, caused by the Junin virus transmitted by infected rodents. It causes fever, haemorrhagic manifestations, low blood counts, and neurological symptoms with a mortality of 15-30% without treatment.

faqs.question: How is Junin hemorrhagic fever transmitted?
faqs.answer: JHF is transmitted to humans by contact with the urine or excreta of the drylands cricetine rodent (Calomys musculinus), primarily during the maize harvest. Transmission occurs through broken skin, mucous membranes, or inhalation of aerosolised contaminated material. Rare person-to-person nosocomial transmission occurs via needlestick or mucous membrane exposure to patient blood.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include fever, severe malaise, headache, myalgia, gingival bleeding, petechiae, ecchymoses, proteinuria, and neurological signs (tremor, ataxia, seizures). Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia are characteristic laboratory findings.

faqs.question: How is Junin hemorrhagic fever diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by RT-PCR for Junin virus RNA in the first week of illness, or ELISA for Junin virus-specific IgM antibodies from day 7 onwards. CBC showing leukopenia and thrombocytopenia is characteristic.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Treatment includes intravenous ribavirin (most effective within first 6 days) and convalescent immune plasma from recovered patients. Supportive care with blood product transfusion, seizure management, and ICU support is critical. Early treatment reduces mortality from 15-30% to 1-5%.

faqs.question: Can Junin hemorrhagic fever be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes. With early treatment (ribavirin and immune plasma initiated within the first week), survival rates exceed 95%. Most survivors recover completely without long-term sequelae.

faqs.question: What is the prognosis for Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: With early treatment, prognosis is excellent with survival rates of 95-99%. Without treatment, mortality is 15-30%. Poor prognostic factors include delayed treatment, neurological involvement, severe thrombocytopenia, and advanced age.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Yes. The live attenuated Candid#1 vaccine is used in Argentina for high-risk agricultural workers in endemic areas. It has demonstrated 80-95% efficacy in clinical trials. It is not available commercially outside Argentina.

faqs.question: Is Junin hemorrhagic fever contagious between people?
faqs.answer: Person-to-person transmission is rare but documented in healthcare settings via needlestick or mucous membrane exposure to patient blood. Standard and droplet precautions prevent nosocomial spread. Casual contact does not transmit the disease.

faqs.question: Where is Junin hemorrhagic fever found?
faqs.answer: JHF is endemic to the pampas region of central Argentina, specifically the provinces of Buenos Aires, Cordoba, Santa Fe, and La Pampa. Seasonal peak occurs during the autumn maize harvest (March-July).

faqs.question: What causes Junin hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: JHF is caused by Junin virus, an arenavirus maintained in nature by the drylands cricetine rodent (Calomys musculinus). Humans are infected through contact with rodent excreta. The virus causes immune activation, capillary leak, and coagulopathy.

faqs.question: How long is the incubation period for JHF?
faqs.answer: The incubation period is 7-14 days from exposure to onset of symptoms. Most cases develop symptoms within 10 days.

faqs.question: Is there a risk of JHF for tourists visiting Argentina?
faqs.answer: The risk for tourists is very low unless they visit endemic rural areas during the harvest season and engage in agricultural work. Standard precautions and avoidance of rodent exposure prevent infection.

faqs.question: What should healthcare workers do if they suspect JHF?
faqs.answer: Isolate the patient immediately with contact and droplet precautions, notify public health authorities, and initiate ribavirin if available. Avoid procedures generating aerosols. Draw blood for diagnostics and handle all specimens with BSL-4 precautions.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Junin and Machupo hemorrhagic fever?
faqs.answer: Both are South American arenaviral haemorrhagic fevers with similar clinical features. Junin is endemic to Argentina; Machupo is endemic to Bolivia. Distinction is made by geography, RT-PCR, and serology. Treatment is identical for both.

faqs.question: Does ribavirin work against all haemorrhagic fevers?
faqs.answer: Ribavirin has broad antiviral activity against several RNA viruses including arenaviruses (Junin, Machupo, Lassa) and some filoviruses. It is not effective against all haemorrhagic fevers (e.g., Ebola data are conflicting). Its efficacy is highest when given early.

faqs.question: What is immune plasma for JHF?
faqs.answer: Immune plasma is plasma collected from patients who have recovered from JHF and contain neutralising antibodies against Junin virus. When administered early, it dramatically reduces mortality. Availability is limited to endemic areas and reference centres.

faqs.question: Can JHF cause long-term health problems?
faqs.answer: Most JHF survivors recover completely without long-term sequelae. A small proportion (5-10%) have persistent neurological symptoms (tremor, cognitive impairment) or fatigue for weeks to months after recovery.

faqs.question: How is JHF prevented?
faqs.answer: Prevention includes: vaccination of at-risk workers with Candid#1; use of PPE (gloves, masks) during harvest; rodent control; and strict infection control in hospitals managing cases.

faqs.question: Is Junin hemorrhagic fever more deadly than Ebola?
faqs.answer: No. Untreated JHF has a 15-30% mortality compared to Ebola's 50-70% case fatality rate. With early treatment, JHF mortality falls to 1-5%, far better than Ebola. Both require BSL-4 containment and similar supportive care.
